column121: In descent from 9000 to 7000 with autopilot engaged hit by moderate jolt of wake turbulence. Continued descent and aircraft began to roll past 30 degrees angle of bank. Autopilot disengaged and aircraft rolled upright. Autopilot reengaged. Checked on flight attendants; all were ok. No passengers were up at the time. Enquired ATC about who we were following. Appeared to be a B777 ahead going to a different runway.
column123: While descending from 9;000 to 7;000 we were given a turn to 170 for vectors to intercept the ILS 9R. In the turn; we experienced wake turbulence - with a bank angle approximately 30 to the right. The Captain disengaged the autopilot and hand flew the airplane back to level flight. I checked on the flight attendants to make sure they were all ok because it was so abrupt and sudden. All 4 of the flight attendants reported to me they were fine. Within a couple of minutes at 7;000'; we encountered wake turbulence again. This time it was a 40 - 45 degree bank angle to the right. My Captain was still hand flying and recovered the airplane. I asked ATC what type of aircraft we were following; the controller responded we weren't following anyone but there was a 777 ahead of us but 2;000' below us. The 777 was being vectored ahead of us for [RWY] 10C. Perhaps more spacing was required even though we were being vectored for different but parallel runways.

column121: I was working Local Control which manages the class D during north flow operations. Aircraft X had been doing air work at an adjacent airport which is about 8 to 9 miles west of this airport. Aircraft Y was inbound on the ILS. Aircraft Z and Aircraft A were inbound from the southwest. Approach called for a point out to Flight Data/Clearance delivery on Aircraft X and when asked what he was going to do we were told he was going back towards the adjacent airport. Aircraft X entered from the southwest and made maneuvers about 2 to 3 southwest at 2;300 feet and then turned southeast directly overflying Aircraft Y on the ILS by 400 feet. Aircraft Y was issued traffic alerts and broken off the approach to a 090 heading. This put them in conflict with Aircraft B; inbound from the southeast. Another traffic alert was given to Aircraft Y for Aircraft B. Just before Aircraft Y was broken off the ILS approach; Aircraft Z a VFR inbound from the southwest was issued a traffic alert since Aircraft X initially turned opposite direction to them. Aircraft A VFR from the southwest also had to be diverted to the east prior to Aircraft Y being broken off the approach because Aircraft X's intentions were unpredictable and not as described. When asked why flight check was turning east (right instead of left) toward our traffic we were told by approach that 'it is what it is.'Flight Check operations are a high risk to anyone else operating in the area. When they arrive their intentions are incredibly vague other than what they are checking. They often seem to just do whatever they want and let you know as they are doing it. From a local standpoint I think that flight check needs to be a lot more clear of their exact intentions) while operating in the vicinity. VFR arrivals need to be rerouted or placed in holding by approach to a different quadrant other than the one that Flight Check is operating in. This is not the first close call Flight Check has caused over the past few years.

column121: At approximately XA00 local time; at ZZZ at an uncontrolled field; I taxied out to the threshold of RWY XXL in biplane single place open cockpit with no Radio. The Beech pilot initiated a go-around to an uneventful landing; announcing that a runway incursion had occurred. Aircraft separation was estimated as several hundred yards; and no damage was incurred by either Aircraft. Difficulty in seeing a blue and white aircraft with blue sky background; and the Aircraft was not flying the designated traffic Encourage all traffic to fly a designated flight pattern and maintain visual awareness and alert to all traffic.

column121: I was on the Local Control position and traffic was light. Initially coordinated with Approach Control; Aircraft X was approved for a late frequency change to tower as it would be number two behind Aircraft Y. With this coordination I was expecting Aircraft Y to be first followed by Aircraft X; but Aircraft X came to tower first approximately two miles north of the VOR. At this time Aircraft Y was still outside of 10 miles from the field and I cleared Aircraft X to land. Approximately on a two mile final; Aircraft X requested a left 360 due to him still being too high. I asked Aircraft X to verify they had the field in sight; but there was no response. The CIC (Controller in Charge) responded to me that he requested a left 360. I observed outside the window that Aircraft X was in his left turn executing a 360 prior to my approval. At this time; Aircraft Y was approximately 8 to 9 nm north of the field. Immediately we coordinated this with Approach letting them know Aircraft X was too high and needed to execute a 360 and was instructed a missed approach; but at the time Aircraft X was just completing his left 360 with Aircraft Y approaching approximately a five mile 5 mile final. With coordination for the missed approach and Aircraft X nearly completing a 360; I advised Aircraft X of the inbound traffic and instructed him to turn northbound and to contact Approach Control. During this time; separation between both aircraft was lost.I believed what led to this event was initially the miscommunication between tower and approach with the sequence of the inbound. I was preparing for Aircraft X to be shipped late behind Aircraft Y; but there wasn't any communication that the sequence has changed.  We are a VFR tower and I haven't had the experience with dealing or the knowledge on how to deal with such a situation; but this incident was definitely a learning experience. I believe Aircraft X also went below the MVA; which is 3000 ft. and they were at 2000 ft. I have not seen or experienced this situation.In my opinion; I believed this occurred because of the miscommunication and both the lack of knowledge from both my position and the pilot in command of Aircraft X. I am aware of the required separation and that IFR procedure. With the last minute decision of Aircraft X to execute the 360 on his own led me to approve his request when I know he must break of the approach and cancel IFR; otherwise they should execute the published missed approach. I would recommend for this VFR tower to include the rare; but possible situations that could occur during training. This situation was unexpected and rare; but it did happen. I would recommend to go over the separation needed for IFR aircraft and the MVAs surrounding the airport. Prior to this situation; I should've noted the altitude; this would have prevented the situation.

column121: The flight was cleared to line up and wait on Runway XL while Aircraft Y was on short final to [Runway] XR. A short time after stopping in position on the runway; the flight was cleared for takeoff.  Takeoff was normal and the flight proceeded to climb to 500 feet and I was just about to initiate the right turn to 060 degrees as required by the ZZZZZ X departure. As I looked to my right; I could see Aircraft Y; on a go-around and apparently flying the runway heading; overtaking us at our altitude and in a position that was; in my opinion; much too close and a very real threat.  Just as this was happening; the tower instructed us in an urgent tone to turn left to heading 360 degrees.  I immediately initiated the turn; hand flying; as the Captain dialed it in with the heading selector. With that action the threat was over; our flight turned away to the left as the Aircraft Y overtook us in both distance and altitude.  The Captain and I learned through the ensuing communication between the Tower and Aircraft Y flight that it had gone around due to a strong wind gust.I have had a considerable amount of experience flying in and out of ZZZ over the years while I was employed by with another company.  It has always been customary that Tower controllers stagger the takeoffs on Runway XL with the landings on XR.  I have noticed that takeoffs usually are cleared as the parallel runway aircraft are either actually landing or rolling out.  This is just what I have noticed through the years and I do not have actual knowledge of Tower procedures at ZZZ.  Because our flight was lined up on Runway XL; I could not see the landing traffic.  When our flight was cleared for takeoff; I made the assumption that the parallel traffic was landing and not going to be a factor.  However; when Aircraft Y went around; it quickly began to overtake us and could have been a real threat to our flight.  With the excellent visibility at the time; I am certain that the Aircraft Y pilots could see us; but it was still very concerning that our two flights got so close.  I am concerned that there was possible loss of situational awareness on the part of the Tower Controller or a deviation from standard ATC procedures.  When Aircraft Y aircraft unexpectedly went around; an unsafe condition could have quickly developed.  This is especially concerning because the SID procedure requires a turn across the parallel runway's extended centerline.I have some concerns that during this COVID pandemic and the resulting slowdown in air traffic; that both flight crews and ATC are no longer operating at the highest levels of attention and situational awareness.  This reinforces in my own mind that absolute adherence to standard operating procedures is so very important and is a major barrier to safety of flight events when unexpected actions occur.  I cannot say for sure; but I believe that our flight was cleared for takeoff too early and when Aircraft Y landing on the parallel runway went around; the situation developed quickly into an unanticipated loss of separation.  I hope that procedures at ZZZ Tower are reviewed and adjusted so that this situation does not develop again.

column121: We were departing ZZZ to ZZZ1 after dropping our passengers off at their personal hangar. As we began taxiing out; we made a call on the CTAF that we were taxiing out and planning to takeoff on RWY XX. We heard an Air Force C130 on CTAF doing work in the pattern. We made a second call as we rounded the hangars on the NE side of the airport. At that time we heard a very faint radio call of a tail number (we could not make out the N#) saying that they 'were entering the pattern; ZZZ' That is all that we heard. I made a radio call asking for the aircraft to repeat the call; and heard nothing. As we were taxiing to the hold short line; I as the PNF (pilot not flying) called for our release from Center Radio. the PF (pilot flying) was monitoring CTAF the entire time; and heard no additional radio calls from the unidentified airplane in the pattern. We stopped at the hold short line and made another radio call that we were holding short and were going to be departing RWY XX. The C130 radio'd that he would extend his orbit so that we could take off. We heard no other radio calls during that time. We crossed the hold short line and I looked and verified the final approach area for RWY XX was clear; the PF looked and verified that RWY XY Final was clear. As we were taxiing into position we heard a very faint 'you guys hold up a minute' call over CTAF. We did not know who it was for and I called over the frequency 'say again' We heard nothing. We started our takeoff run. About 5 seconds later we saw a Cessna landing on RWY XY and aborted our takeoff at a speed of about 100 knots IAS. We called out on the radio that we had made radio calls saying that we were taking off. I do not recall the exact words but we were getting chewed out for taking off while a local was trying to land. We taxed clear of the runway; reset everything; checked our brake temperatures; made another radio call and proceeded to take off uneventfully.We try to be hyper vigilant operating a high performance jet out of uncontrolled fields. It is possible; but I believe unlikely that we missed radio calls from the aircraft in question; but it is possible that we did. I believe the problem arose from a weak transmitter on the Cessna; and non standard phraseology and lack of position report calls on the CTAF. I believe that we followed the AIM guidance for operating on an uncontrolled field; by making several radio calls stating our intentions; and listening for other traffic. We heard no other position reports from the Cessna such as 'entering downwind or turning base' etc. I am not saying the other pilot did not make the calls; I'm saying we did not hear them if he did. There is also a dip in the runway that prevents you from seeing the end of RWY XY from the end of RWY XX. In addition the pilot of the Cessna did not have his landing light on; which would have helped to identify him sooner. We asked for a repeat of his initial radio call; but heard nothing. We also heard no other radio reports from any aircraft other than the C130. In addition; this could have been avoided if a proper radio call such as 'ZZZ Traffic Aircraft Y short final; landing RWY XY ZZZ' had been made instead of 'You guys hold up a minute'. We can always be more vigilant in our actions. Taxiing out while getting a clearance and setting up the FMS is a very busy time; monitoring 2 radios can be distracting. Careful attention to detail and vigilant use of the checklist and PF/PNF duties all need to be done to ensure that nothing is missed; inside and outside the airplane while operating on an uncontrolled field.

column121: I was conducting a flight training event with my student pilot. We just returned to the airport to practice performance takeoff and landings. We had just made a full stop - taxi back. We called holding short that we were ready for touch and goes. We were told to hold short for landing traffic. Several planes land and depart. Both my student and I hear what we believe to be our abbreviated tail number being cleared for takeoff. We read back cleared for takeoff instructions; finish final items on the checklist; and take the runway. On our takeoff roll we hear Tower ask who is on the runway and I respond with our tail number. Tower then advises to either exit the runway or begin our roll. We were already on the roll; so we continued the takeoff. After our lap in the pattern we terminate the training flight and contact the ATC number. I believe I heard our tail number and believed it was a takeoff clearance. However; due to a high volume of traffic the morning of the event due to a lengthy TFR that lasted most of the day; there was a lot of training and jet traffic. I could have heard a similar call sign and my student could have stepped on the intended recipient of the ATC instruction. I know when there is a high volume of traffic waiting to arrive and depart it could be best to use my full tail number to lessen confusion; and take extra measures to ensure all radio communications are properly received and copied.
column123: Aircraft X called up requesting pattern work during a high traffic volume session and was given hold short instructions; due to the fact that there was an IFR arrival jet on about a 3 or 4 mile final. Aircraft X subsequently read back his hold short instructions. I talk to 3 different aircraft before I cleared Aircraft Y for a touch and go. Apparently Aircraft X read back 'Aircraft X cleared touch and go' and then subsequently got onto the runway without me catching it. Aircraft Y clarifies their touch and go clearance and I canceled the touch and go clearance and gave them a left 360. During this time inside the cab my CIC/GC/FD was trying to clarify information about a TFR that was supposed to be going into effect shortly and we had received no prior information on. This resulted in several phones ringing during this already busy time. After scanning the runway I saw an aircraft who appeared to be holding on the runway with my jet traffic now within 2 miles of the runway; since I had multiple aircraft holding short of the runway I tried to quickly identify the aircraft; so I could taxi them off the runway in a timely manner. However Aircraft X didn't identify himself by name until about the 3rd time I reached out to them. With minimum time to react I told the aircraft to taxi clear of the runway or depart immediately. Aircraft X started their departure roll and as soon as airborne I told them to offset the runway so I had sufficient spacing with my IFR aircraft on final. Immediately following my Jet traffic landing the OM entered the cab and started trying to have the CIC help him find our local weather form; so our new trainee could fill it out. We started the day short staffed with no prior knowledge that someone would be coming in to cover for a couple hours; so the rotation wasn't going to happen as expected. When there are less people in the cab we have enough space to work without our masks and due to the fact that there were extra nonessential people in the cab I had to replace my mask; while I firmly believe mask should be required; they do interfere with the side tone which affected my ability to catch Aircraft X's incorrect readback. No one in the facility had received any information on the TFR that was supposed to be going into effect. This resulted in several miscommunications and confusion.

column121: I was the FLM on duty in the Tower cab providing OJTI to a CPC at the time of the incident. I was explaining MORs (Mandatory Occurrence Report) and significant events notifications and showing the CPC where to find the contact numbers. Traffic was light and there were only two positions open as per the COVID staffing; LC1/LC2/LA were combined as CD/FD/GC were also combined. I had one other person on a break.While giving instruction and demonstrating required activity for significant MORs to the CPC; I heard the ASDE-X alarm 'Runway XR go-around' I immediately looked up and scanned Runway XR to assess the situation and determine what caused the alarm. I observed Aircraft Y; 1/2 mile final landing on Runway XR and a small GA aircraft on Taxiway [5] turning South on Taxiway [3]. I didn't observe any other targets or obstructions on the runway surface area. At that time Aircraft Y was already flaring out on the runway. I then asked the LC Controller why wasn't Aircraft Y sent around. The Controller responded; 'The other aircraft cleared the runway.' I then informed the Controller if the safety logic says to send someone around on a runway which the aircraft was landing it was mandatory to comply. The Controller advised me that they thought it was safer to land than send the aircraft around. I then informed the Controller that we are obligated to send the aircraft around per the JO7110.65 and if the pilot decided to land anyway then I would have a conversation with the pilot.Remind controllers that the safety logic overrides same runway separation and that it's mandatory to comply with the safety logic instructions. I did have this conversation with my crew after the incident and was shocked to know they weren't aware of this and thought it was a discretionary decision.

column121: Aircraft Y was cleared for an approach and was cleared to land. Aircraft X called Ground to taxi and was given taxi instructions. This instruction should have resulted in Aircraft X coming out of the Ramp and making a right turn on to the taxiway. Aircraft X instead crossed the taxiway and made a right turn onto the runway. When Aircraft X entered the runway; Aircraft Y immediately stated; 'There's something on the runway;' and I initiated a go around. Aircraft Y went around and conducted another ILS approach to successfully land. Aircraft X exited the runway and continued down the taxiway to Runway 07; where they then departed.Aircraft Y was at 300 ft. at a ½-mile final when the go around was initiated. I asked the pilots of both aircraft to contact the Tower and notified them that I would file a report on the event. I did not say the words 'possible pilot deviation' to Aircraft X; as required to issue a proper brasher warning. I also did not get the pilots' names; phone numbers; and certificate numbers; as I did not know that these items were required until I began filling out the report. I did note on the report that the aircraft were both air carriers; and the information was able to be obtained by referencing the callsigns.I believe the weather was a contributing factor to Aircraft X making an incorrect turn; as it was raining moderately with periodic gusting wind. A ground based radar system or a taller Control Tower would have been incredibly helpful in this circumstance; as the taxiway and runway are pretty much indistinguishable from each other at night. The lights of the two blend together and you cannot tell which is which.

column121: I was working Local Control and Tower CIC.  Aircraft Y was on final to Runway X and Aircraft X was ready to depart Runway Y.  I cleared Aircraft X for takeoff and issued a right turn heading 210.  The proper heading for the departure was 120; per LOA.  I transposed the numbers and missed my error on the readback.  After I observed Aircraft X start his turn; he began to enter a cloud layer and I switched him to departure.  A few moments later; I realized my error upon watching the radar.  I issued a traffic call to Aircraft Y on approach and he called Aircraft X in sight.  No TCAS resolution or CA alert were issued for aircraft proximity.I believe the only recommendations I could make to stop this sort of incident from occurring would be to develop Standard Instrument Departures at ZZZ; which I am not sure is feasible.  Also; I believe the COVID-19 staffing needs to be changed; and more personnel brought back to mitigate the lack of for control positions and lack of oversight. column121: I was about to begin the flight portion of a lesson with my student. We were holding short of runway; allowing multiple planes to safely land before we had a good opportunity to depart. While holding short; another plane had landed and was about to pull off of the runway onto A3; but there was an Aircraft Y on the other side of the hold short line. A radio call was made asking what the Aircraft Y was doing; and then he taxied out of A3 and Aircraft Z was able to taxi back to midfield parking. After Aircraft Z was clear of the runway; another aircraft landed on Runway 18; did a touch and go; and took off for a south departure. After waiting for about 5 aircraft to land on 18; the traffic pattern was now not busy; there was one aircraft on the downwind. I was positioned to where I could see the upwind; left downwind; left base and final of the traffic pattern; as well as the entire runway and taxiway and determined it safe to now make my radio call announcing my departure. After the radio call was made; it was not blocked; again I visually inspected that the runway was clear and slowly rolled onto 18. It was my student's second flight lesson; and his mother accompanied us in the backseat. While talking him through how to taxi onto the runway; how to takeoff and where to be visually scanning; I performed a normal takeoff; lifting off at rotation speed; the runway was still clear. After about five to ten seconds after rotation; I looked down to see my ground track over the runway and saw Aircraft Y directly beneath me. While maintaining positive control of the aircraft; talking through what I was doing with my student I flew at the speed that safely allowed us to gain as altitude. I made a radio call asking what the pilot in the Aircraft Y was doing and if he had not heard or seen us take off and if he had us in sight at that moment. He did not respond. Then I said 'What the hell dude?' on the radio. Due to how fast everything happened; and trying to remain calm and fly the airplane safely; it was hard to determine exactly how close the aircraft actually was to me. At this point I determined the safest action was to get us as far away from this plane as I could. I continued on the upwind; to where I could not see the Aircraft Y anymore. I was hoping that he had aborted his takeoff. Continuing out and scanning; maintaining a calm demeanor for myself as well as my passengers; I then heard an early crosswind call and knew it was the pilot in the Aircraft Y. Another instructor from FBO said '[name]; what is going on with you today?' A few other aircraft responded 'Agreed;' 'Agreed.' After this I did not want to return to the pattern knowing the Aircraft Y was still flying. I decided to take my student to the practice area to where I could continue my lesson and also monitor the traffic pattern to know when it would be safe to return; as I always do. After about 30 minutes I determined it was safe to return to the airport and land; because I didn't hear or see anyone in the traffic pattern. We landed safely. My passengers did not even notice what was happening until we were almost clear of the traffic pattern; I had told them someone was trying to takeoff underneath us. Still; they do not know the severity of how close it was because of being distracted by it being one of their first flights and not paying attention to the radio calls or doing the constant visual scan. I am extremely fortunate that my passengers and I are alive. This pilot; name; is the definition of unsafe. He chooses to make extremely unsafe actions. I will never fly if I see this man at the airport and will tell every student I have and pilot I see to also not fly if this man is out. If he is not held accountable for his actions; I fear that someone could be tragically hurt. My coworker; another CFI was a witness standing at midfield parking near A3 taxiway; and said it was a miss of 5 feet.

column121: While I was training on Local during the busiest session since returning to training aswell as it being my trainer's first time training on local in more than 6 months thetraffic volume was enough that required us to split Local and Ground. For most of theyear our airport had been on a straight runway configuration due to construction and we had only been back to 2 runway operations for about a week. We had Aircraft Zdoing full stop taxi backs and Aircraft A doing stop and go's with afternoon push ofarrivals and departures. I had cleared Aircraft Y to land on runway YY (IFR arrival) andAircraft X was ready to go on runway XX (VFR departure). I asked Aircraft X if heneeded time on the runway or if he was ready to go and the pilot replied that he wasready to go. When I cleared Aircraft X for takeoff the Aircraft Z was on 2.5 mile finaland traffic was exchanged. After takeoff clearance I went back and told Aircraft X toclear for immediate takeoff and the pilot read it back. After 30 seconds the departingaircraft had not began its takeoff roll yet and my instructor told them to start roll now.At this time the arrival was at 1 mile final runway YY. When the arrival was at thethreshold the departure had barely cleared the intersection due to the delay it madeon the runway.To prevent this situation I could have waited until the arrival had landed to clear thedeparture for takeoff. I relied on expectation bias that the departure was going tobegin their takeoff roll immediately and not take time on the runway. I could havealso told the Aircraft Y to reduce to final approach speed to create a bigger hole forthe departure. Knowing when to slow down during busy sessions is also somethingthat could have prevented this situation.
column123: (Training in progress on Local Control on one of the busier sessions seen by all inmonths. Trainer's first time training on LC in over 9 months)Ground had been split off a couple minutes before due to increased traffic andproposed traffic. In addition to multiple arrivals and departures; there was Aircraft Zdoing multiple take offs with full stop taxi backs; and Aircraft A doing stop & go's.Aircraft X taxied out for departure contacted tower holding short of Runway XX. Trafficwas within a mile final for Runway XX so they were waiting to depart. Because therewas another aircraft inbound to Runway YY that we were going to try to depart insideof; Local Trainee was instructed to ask Aircraft X if they would need time on therunway or if they would be ready to go-they said they would be ready. Once the Stopand Go traffic was rolling and we would have our separation; Aircraft X was cleared fortakeoff and told about traffic on final for crossing runway. No hustle from aircraft.Trainee then told Aircraft X cleared for immediate takeoff. He still sat on the runway.Trainer keyed up and told Aircraft X to start their roll.The runway separation was extremely close to call and in the moment the trainerdecided that it was safer to let the arrival land than issue a go around and potentiallycause an evasive maneuver at a critical stage of flight with another departure.We should have just waited to clear Aircraft X for takeoff due to their tendency to beslow and spend time on the runway. Once they did not move with an immediatetakeoff; take off clearance should have been canceled.I do believe that traffic volume and complexity added and distracted the tower-teamfrom checking every aspect of the situation (speeds; timing; rolling of aircraft); but Ido not believe we should refrain from training on busy sessions like these.

column121: We were taxing for departure. A Citation reported entering a right downwind and [the F/O (First Officer)] announced taxiing to the runway and that [we] would hold in the run-up area for the landing Citation. Upon reaching the run-up area I set the parking brake and proceeded to accomplish the pre-takeoff run-up checks. While parked well clear of the approach end of runway heard the Citation pilot announce right base. I commented that I did not see the Citation and [the F/O] indicated that he saw it just above the trees I looked in the general direction and saw the aircraft and commented to [the F/O] that it appeared to be slightly right of the centerline and he agreed. As the aircraft got closer it became readily apparent that the aircraft was lined up with the taxiway on which we were parked and was going to pass dangerously close if it continued on its current trajectory. [The F/O] admonished the Citation pilot to go around 3 times. The aircraft did not deviate. Fearing a collision I advanced the power levers; simultaneously released the parking brake and [as] the aircraft moved forward the left main gear exited the tarmac and the left prop struck an elevated reflector. The Citation passed overhead over the aft portion of the aircraft at what I estimated to be approximately 20-25 ft. above our aircraft and appeared to be initiating a go around. I heard the Citation pilot call 'Going around' and admonishing us for being on the runway at which point first [the F/O] and myself corrected him by telling him he had just overflown us on the taxiway he then apologized saying something about no runway lights. I taxied the aircraft to parking shut down; called Maintenance. A combination of inoperative runway edge lighting; the Citation pilot's decision to continue approach and attempt to land with inadequate lighting and failure to execute a go-around until prompted on the CTAF frequency. The damage to [our aircraft] was the direct result of rapid evasive maneuvering to avoid a possible collision with the Citation attempting to land on the taxiway.

column121: Aircraft X [flight] got handed off from ZLC they passed along that they were low fuel. Aircraft X was a flight of 6. With the weather being poor and the visibility being 4 miles an instrument approach is what they were requesting. That being said; their TACAN and ILS were both out of service. The only way they were getting in was a visual to [Runway] XXR. So I vectored the flight behind the [civilian] aircraft coming from the south. Giving them block altitudes above the MVA so they had proper spacing amongst themselves. As I was stepping Aircraft X down they kept wanting lower and lower in order to get the visual approach. I stepped them down to 5000 and they descended to 4800; I gave them an LOA (low altitude alert). Then after fixing that issue I gave them 4500 once they were in the 4500 MVA. Turned them to final and I noticed that they dropped below 4500. So I gave them another LOA and told them to maintain 4500. I asked them numerous times if they had the airport in sight. They said no so I climbed them to 8000 and told them to fly heading 100. Aircraft X flight 2-5 reported the field in sight and at that point I cleared them for the visual. Aircraft X 6 didn't get the airport so I turned him away from the mountains and climbed them. After that I gave him a new squawk code and split Aircraft X 1 and Aircraft X 6 up. After clearing Aircraft X 6 to ZZZ and verifying his altitude I sequenced the two flights to get the visual behind another arrival. When we have airplanes that are low fuel and have no approaches we need to get those approaches fixed. Tech ops or who ever was fixing them need to understand the situation on hand.

column121: I am a flight instructor I was on the ground observing the traffic pattern. I had just sent my student on his first solo flight. There were three Citabrias in the pattern; all same company. My solo student was Aircraft X. Aircraft Z was on ½ mile final. Aircraft Y was on an extended downwind. My student; Aircraft X; was abeam midfield in the downwind. All traffic was in a left pattern. Tower called and told my student 'traffic to follow ahead in the downwind'. My student replied with 'traffic in sight'. My student had mistaken Aircraft Z on final as the traffic he was following; instead and of seeing and following Aircraft Y in the downwind. Tower proceeded to clear my student 'follow that traffic; clear to land Runway XX'. Because my student was following the wrong aircraft; he turned base (about a half mile) in front of Aircraft Y and cut him off. Aircraft Y was on a 1 mile final at the time. Aircraft Y saw the error and made appropriate maneuvers. Tower noticed when Aircraft X had turned final in front of Aircraft Y and proceeded to tell both aircraft to go around. No further events took place. The pilot in Aircraft X did not see or follow the correct traffic and turned base accordingly. He unintentionally deviated from the ATC clearance. The plot of Aircraft Y noticed and took evasive actions as necessary. I was on the ground and all distances are highly estimated.
column123: I was given traffic information by ATC while on downwind leg of pattern; a Aircraft X in the pattern ahead and to my left. I saw an aircraft matching ATC description on short final approach and responded that I had the traffic in sight. ATC gave landing clearance with instructions to follow that aircraft; so when I had sufficient distance behind I began my turn to the base leg of the pattern. Upon turning I spotted another aircraft ahead and to my right at about 500 feet clearance on final approach. I took a hard left turn to evade and was told by ATC to go around and that the aircraft I flew in front of was the traffic I was originally advised of and that I had cut them off. I continued in the traffic pattern and landed without further incident.

column121: Our aircraft had just landed at ZZZ when a single-seat aerobatic biplane began to do low-level aerobatics (hammerheads and rolls) opposite normal traffic in the pattern. A Cessna 150 entered left downwind Runway XX; well spaced from the biplane; which was in a modified right downwind/base; when the biplane crossed the field and entered a midfield left crosswind-to-downwind behind the 150. The 150 continued downwind; turned base then final; when the biplane banked hard from downwind directly to final abeam the 150 and approximately 25 feet above. The 150 initiated a go-around and the biplane did the same; nearly colliding which each other. The 150 eventually landed on a second go-around while the biplane continued to execute high speed low passes over the runway at about 50 feet AGL. The problem likely arose because the biplane; which was NORDO; was engaged in a low level acrobatics display for a group of onlookers in the tiedown area and was not scanning for conflicting traffic. The 150 likely saw the biplane traffic near the airport; but the biplane's erratic behavior and rapid altitude and direction changes would have complicated efforts to maintain separation. The conflict could have been resolved if the biplane pilot refrained from low-level aerobatics in the vicinity of the traffic pattern; was visually scanning for traffic; and vacated the traffic pattern area when other traffic was inbound to land.

column121: What happened: Aircraft Y was given a take off clearance for a runway that was occupied by a helicopter that had landed on the opposite end and was awaiting taxi instructions.What led to this:  The helicopter was coming in from the east and requested to land on the western side of the airport.  The active runways at the time were south to north (XXL and XXR).  The Tower cleared the helicopter to land on the numbers of an inactive runway XY; which would keep the helicopter on the eastern side of the airport; away from the flow of fixed wing traffic. About 5 minutes elapsed between the time the helicopter landed and Aircraft Y flew over the helicopter.During that time I believe the Controller may have been distracted by pattern traffic; Aircraft Z arrival (this was a factor prior to the helicopter landing which resulted in the controller deciding to keep the helicopter on the eastern side of the airport); a change in Aircraft Y IFR clearance; and by a pilot of an airplane who mistakenly made a final approach to the wrong parallel runway.Starting from the point at which the helicopter lands; the following exchanges were heard.Aircraft A was cleared for touch and go #2 behind Aircraft Z for runway XXL.Aircraft B was cleared to Land runway XXR.A few seconds after Aircraft B acknowledged the landing clearance the tower issue a taxi instruction to the airborne Aircraft B 'hold short of runway XXR I am going to have a departure off runway XZ.'Aircraft B responded 'I am in the air.'The tower replied 'Disregard; Land and hold short of runway XZ; land and hold short of runway XZ.'Aircraft B responded that this was their intention and would be parking in an area that would not put them at risk of crossing into runway XZ.The tower approved a request to taxi back to parking after Aircraft B completed a 180 degree turn on the runway after landing.30 seconds later the tower controller said to Aircraft B; 'Disregard the land and hold short clearance. I won't have that departure.  He will be holding in place; though.'The tower instructed Aircraft Y to line up and wait on runway XZ.The 737 had already landed on XXL and the tower cleared a Cessna for takeoff runway XXL.20 seconds later Aircraft B realized he was about to land on top of Aircraft A who had just been cleared for takeoff and asked the tower if he should go around.  There was no response.  Aircraft B hailed the tower again.  I observed Aircraft B descend below 100 ft AGL before deciding to go around.  40 seconds elapsed between the 1st and 3rd time Aircraft B called the tower.The tower gave the departing Aircraft A; a mixed up call starting with 'line up and- cancel take off- cleared for takeoff runway XXL'The tower then gave Aircraft B instructions to remain in the upwind; he would give him a crosswind instruction to follow.Aircraft B apologized for lining up to land on the wrong runway.As soon as the frequency congestion allowed; knowing there was a jet on the opposite side of my runway; I made a call 'Tower; Helicopter (call-sign) holding position on the numbers of [Runway] XY ready to taxi to (name of ramp)'.  The call was not acknowledged and 55 seconds later an amended departure clearance; combined with a takeoff clearance; was given to the waiting Aircraft Y.   I did not hear the departure clearance and Aircraft Y began racing towards us.  Ironically; I may have turned to my student and said to keep an eye on Aircraft Y.  As it rushed overhead I made an aggressive maneuver off of the numbers and onto the closest available vacant spot beyond the runway safety area.Previous experiences at this airport have made it clear when a helicopter accepts a landing clearance to a spot; taxiway; intersection; or helipad; they are expected to remain there until given a clearance to taxi.  Quite often the controllers will vector an arriving helicopter far around the approach or departure center lines of the active runways and restrict them to altitudes below 600 MSL instead of accommodating a flight path crossing the active runways at a higher safe altitude. Less often are we allowed to land as in the case above in a place safely away from the departing/arriving aircraft; and in a position where we can easily air taxi at a moment's notice.  A helicopter is highly maneuverable and this aspect should be used to the utmost advantage when minimizing delays to arriving or departing airplanes.  As mentioned earlier the helicopter's arrival was prior to Aircraft Z checking in with the tower.  There was Aircraft A on a half-mile final to runway XXL ahead of Aircraft Z.  With several miles of spacing between Aircraft Z and Aircraft A; the tower began to ask the helicopter to pass behind the landing Aircraft A.  We accepted.  The tower immediately reversed their decision due to Aircraft Z scheduled arrival and cleared us to land on the numbers of [Runway] XY with the restriction 'remain north of XX'. We accepted the clearance to land.  It is very likely that a helicopter crossing the approach end of XXL shortly after the landing Aircraft A would have had little to no effect on any of the other present arriving or departing aircraft.

column121: On date I was conducting training in an R22 Helicopter departing out of ZZZ at XA:45 local time to ZZZ1. Winds were predominantly out of the west at around 10 kts and we joined left closed traffic for RWY 26 at ZZZ1. There was high training volume and I estimate 7 aircraft were on the ground waiting to depart or actively in the Right Closed Traffic for RWY XX plus our Helicopter. At around XB:10 local time a Jet taxied to the active with no Radio (or no radio communication was made) as we were on a left downwind for [Runway] XX. Local pilots made calls on the radio so all would be aware of his departure and be vigilant/use caution. The jet took off without incident and departed to the North. As pilots worked together to sequence traffic we made our base and final leg before again departing XX to rejoin left closed traffic. At approximately XB:15 local time on the upwind of XX roughly 300ft AGL without warning the jet made a low pass on runway XY at very high speed. I would estimate the pilot was 300ft off our starboard side and about 100ft below our current altitude. I was able to track his flight after the incident to find his Ground Speed was in excess of 330mph. The Jet than proceeded back out to the North again and ended up circling to land on RWY XY this time to a full stop. (The following is my personal opinion) I believe this pilot does not have any remorse for his action. He has an attitude that the FAA can't/won't get him in trouble for this reckless behavior. He wasn't breaking any REG by operating without a radio in an ECHO airspace but his speed certainly was in violation of FAR 91.117(a) and given the high training volume being conducted on this day the pilot showed a clear disregard for other pilots or even his/her own safety.

column121: Flight was from ZZZ to ZZZ2 with a stop to pick up a passenger at ZZZ1. ZZZ Approach provided flight following all the way to ZZZ1 as I was unfamiliar with the airport and had difficulty identifying it from the air. After overflying the field at 2;000 ft (Approach assigned flight following altitude) a frequency change to the CTAF was approved and made. I descended to pattern altitude and crossed midfield before entering downwind leg for runway XX. Crossing midfield; turning to downwind; base and final; position reports were made on the CTAF. No other aircraft operating at ZZZ1 were heard on frequency. Landing XX; proceeded to back taxi to the end of the runway. At the XY threshold; my passenger saw me and began approaching the aircraft. So as to not create an undue hazard to my passenger; I exited the runway onto the available taxiway and turned parallel to XY. At this point; my left wingtip was impinging on the runway.I continued to hear no other aircraft on frequency as I performed a safety briefing for my passenger and ensured he was ready to fly. Shortly thereafter; another aircraft landed on runway 19. This aircraft proceeded to back taxi on XY and then made contact on CTAF. The other aircraft pilot reported to have been making calls on CTAF and attempting to reach me prior to landing; but I did not hear him. The other aircraft pilot reported landing left of center due to the runway incursion. On resuming flight towards ZZZ2 another aerodrome on the same CTAF frequency performed a radio check with me and assured that they could receive me loud and clear.

column121: High speed abort.Captain at controls.Cleared for takeoff and Tower advised aircraft on 5 mile final as we approached the RWY (Runway). Approximately 1/4 way down runway into our takeoff roll; Challenger announced going around left side. At approximately 1/2 way down runway into our takeoff roll; Tower directed us to abort our takeoff. We were in the high-speed regime at approximately 115 kts. I could see the jet going around on our left abeam; in my peripheral vision as we were aborting.Maximum reverse application w/ maximum brakes. Uneventful abort; cleared runway onto Taxiway F and brought jet to a stop. Manually held brakes while running all applicable checklists. 2 MLG (Main Landing Gear) trucks reached 610 C; others approximately 590 C. Brake temp cooling chart referenced 35 mins used; brakes took approximately 50 min to cool to 270 C. In the meantime; ZZZ Fire-Rescue examined MLG. No evidence of overheat. Conferred with [Maintenance Control] for any further guidance. Later; Maintenance came to the jet; examined MLG per [Maintenance Control] guidance and gave us a thumbs up.In the meantime; we had a conference call with Flight Ops Management and ZZZ Station Ops. Both gave us the ok to continue'.Maintenance Control sent us a new maintenance release; Dispatch sent Release 3. Brake temp left main was the highest temp; at approximately 240 C when we began taxi. No noticeable increases.We departed ZZZ; kept gear down for approximately 5 minutes til all MLG brakes were below 200 C and decreasing. Retracted gear and continued to ZZZ1 via the flight plan.As a side-note; we received an ACARS message at approximately 7;000 ft.; on climbout; directing us to return to gate for another check. We leveled at 9;000 ft.; called Dispatch for clarification. He talked to Maintenance Control and surmised the return to gate message was an old message and may have been caught up in the ACARS system somehow and decided to transmit to us after takeoff roll. Anyhow; message was deemed as received in error and we got a thumbs up to continue OPS (Operations) normal to ZZZ1. I'm not sure if this is something systemic to Company 2 ACARS interface or not; but this issue might need to be examined."
column123: Cleared takeoff at ZZZ with aircraft on 5 nm final. As we began our roll I heard the aircraft on final say they were initiating a go-around. Around 115 KIAS the Tower directed us to reject which we accomplished. We told the passengers to remain seated and we taxied clear to find a place to cool our brakes; get Maintenance to inspect our brakes/tires; and coordinate for a new maintenance release and Dispatch release while re-initializing our FMC. We accomplished all pre-flight; before push and before takeoff checklists again and accomplished an uneventful takeoff over an hour later and landing uneventfully in ZZZ1.

column121: The timing of the situation was ripe for a mistake; as a similar-sounding call sign received the exact type of clearance we would have expected as we approached the end of the runway while I was task saturated with completing the required checklist. Ideally; I would not have misheard the call sign; but ultimately I should have realized my attention had been entirely focused on the checklist; not the radio; and asked Tower for a short delay; clarification; or repeated clearance. We were operating the second leg of the day as Aircraft X from ZZZ to ZZZ1. We were given a short taxi from the terminal to Runway XXR. Approaching the departure end of XXR I switched to Tower frequency while still completing the Before Takeoff Checklist. As we made the turn from B onto B5 and while still finishing the last few steps of the checklist; Tower cleared another aircraft with a similar-sounding call sign; to line up and wait on XXC. I mistakenly thought the line up and wait clearance was for us and read back a clearance to line up and wait on Runway] XXR. Tower did not acknowledge my call; and while the Captain and I both saw an aircraft on final for [Runway] XXR; it appeared there was adequate spacing for the line up and wait clearance to be reasonable. As our aircraft passed the hold short line; Tower cleared the similar call sign for takeoff on XXC. The Captain and I simultaneously realized there had been a possible mix up of call signs. We advised tower we had read back the line up and wait clearance and asked for confirmation that it had been for us. Tower advised that it had not been for us; but cleared us for an immediate takeoff to accommodate the aircraft on final for [Runway] XXR. We immediately transitioned to a rolling takeoff and were able to get airborne before any go-around or other action was required by the aircraft on final.
column123: It did not sound right to me when the Tower said to line up and wait; but after the First Officer read back the instructions and the Tower did not correct us we pressed on. Next time I am in a situation that does not feel right; I will stop what I am doing and verify the instructions before taking the runway.We were taxiing to Runway XXR in ZZZ. As we were approaching the hold short line we heard the instruction to line up and wait. The First Officer read back 'Aircraft X line up and wait XXR.' Both of us looked at final approach and saw an  aircraft on approximately a 5 mile final; I verified this with my MFD. As we turned on to the runway the Tower cleared an aircraft for takeoff that had a very similar call sign as ours. At this point; I verified our instructions to lineup and wait. Tower said that he did not clear us to line up and wait. The Controller immediately cleared us for takeoff and so we took off.

We're reduced staffing due to COVID-19; and having to train forces us to combine positions to accomplish the numbers that management is pushing for.

column121: Landed Runway 35R and was instructed by DFW tower to taxi Q - Z hold short of Runway 35C. Since we were already moving; I determined it would be safer to continue across the runway and exit rather than stop in the runway environment. Notified Tower Controller once clear of the Runway that the REL's illuminated as we crossed Runway 35C. The Controller apologized and stated that was not supposed to happen since they were in Land and Hold short (LAHSO) operations.Possible ground equipment failure or programming error.Not certain. Perhaps a question for DFW Tower.

Landing traffic was still airborne on approach and we were already across the hold short line; so we decided it was safest to continue and exit the runway. column121: I had taken position after coming down from the tower working Ground Control. A priority handling had been coordinated with Aircraft X with an engine out. This had been coordinated at least 45 minutes prior to the aircraft's arrival. It was briefed from the relieved controller that this aircraft had requested 'wide vectors for an ILS.' At the same time Aircraft Z was inbound on the RNAV arrival along with Aircraft Y call sign unknown. In my judgement as well as the relieved Controller both of these aircraft would have arrived easily before Aircraft X came in on the ILS. I cleared Aircraft Y for the Visual Approach to the right parallel runway and decided to change the Aircraft Y to the left after they requested the ILS. Aircraft X changed his mind for the Visual Approach which I approved. I asked Aircraft X if they could visually follow the Aircraft Y and they said they would not because of wake turbulence. Since I did not issue a 30 degree intercept we coordinated with the tower to issue a go-around to Aircraft Y. We decided not to send the Aircraft Y around because he was established on the ILS even though I had not issued a 30 degree intercept for Aircraft X. In my opinion wake turbulence to a priority handling aircraft was more of a factor than a parallel runway approach.Due to the busy nature of this session I think splitting the radar position may have been warranted. The relieved Controller had been on position for over 1 hour and 45 minutes. Aircraft X changing his mind mid sequence also caused a lot of problems. I was wary of issuing delay vectors to an aircraft that was a priority handling.

column121: Prior to the event; I came up from break and took a LC (Local Control) position that controls a satellite Class D airport with a gravel strip and water lanes. This position was combined with both CIC and Clearance Delivery/FD. This was never trained as a standard; but some work crews have made this a normal practice when staffing is short. Quickly after taking position; and before the event occurred; I did call up someone to split off the position but I should not have allowed it to remain de-combined in the interim. LC and GC (Ground Control) were de-combined; and while working I heard GC bring a potential conflict to LCs attention (good tower teamwork from GC). As I examined the situation I saw the same conflict- a heavy aircraft had taxied into departure position (Runway 33) with a large aircraft arriving behind on a non-intersecting runway (Runway 7L). With the spacing of the aircraft it did not appear that there was enough space to ensure compliance with our facility order. Our facility order details spacing required with aircraft arriving Runways 7R/L and departing Runway 33. I advised the LC that I did not believe adequate spacing would exist and suggested; but did not instruct; alternate courses of action to avoid a situation where the arriving aircraft might encounter jet blast. The LC insisted it would work and did not take any action. With the proximity of the aircraft; I don't believe a safety issue existed; however lack of compliance with facility guidelines with no standard for separation is an unacceptable risk to the users of the system.This event highlights many issues.As CIC during the event; I take responsibility for my own personal performance. I should not have taken CIC combined with an LC position. I should have had more engagement in the operation - if this situation had been detected earlier there may have been more time to correct it. I also should have given the LC instruction instead of suggestion when I saw action was not going to be taken.1. Lack of staffing. Perpetual lack of staffing leads this facility down the path of using what should be contingency practices into day to day practices. Positions are too often combined and because this has become the norm; controllers do not understand that they are providing a reduced level of service.2. Lack of safety culture. LC had two controllers address concern over this action and did not appear to consider an alternate course of action. This is not a behavior to this specific Controller but throughout this facility.3. Lack of training. The training culture and training at this facility has fallen short on ensuring that the staff of the facility have a thorough understanding of the rules we are expected to apply.4. Lack of supervision. Much like our Controller workforce; our supervisory staff is frequently understaffed and asked to do an excessive amount of duties that take them away from the operation. There was not a supervisor present at the time of the incident.5. Bad procedures. The facility; and region as a whole have done a poor job at creating procedures that are not easily followed by pilots or controllers. Many of these procedures don't have reasonable 'outs'; which put pilots and controllers in dangerous situations in a recovery situation.Consider revising ANC jet blast procedures.Provide clear expectations to CPCs on services we are expected to provide pilots.Provide adequate staffing to accomplish existing goals.

column121: DEN ground instructed us to taxi to RWY 17L from E4 via mike; hold short of ED. Once on Mike northbound Ground instructed us to turn right at ED and hold short of the approach end of Runway 17R. The FO (First Officer) was in the process of completing his delayed engine start flow as I started the turn on ED. At this point I noticed traffic on short final to Runway 17R and thinking to myself that it didn't seem right to turn on ED. I stopped the aircraft as soon as it was straight and pointed east on ED. I asked the FO if he saw a sign or hold short line on his side. I saw no signage in front of us and now felt extremely uncomfortable with our proximity to the Runway 17R approach path. At this point the Ground Controller told us we were supposed to stop 50 ft. further back. I looked behind us and noticed the left wing tip was over the east edge of Taxiway Mike. I could not see a hold line below the wing. We were switched to Tower. The Tower Controller gave us a number to call for a possible pilot deviation. Shortly after writing down the number we were cleared for takeoff. As we rolled down the runway I realized we failed to complete the final items.I spoke to the Tower supervisor later that day and explained the events as they occurred. He admitted that this hot spot is an ongoing issue; the hold area is not well marked and the Controller's intent was to have us pointed east to help move aircraft through the Runway 17R approach path faster. Unfortunately the Tower had to send around the landing traffic I was distracted with during the turn because they were not sure if we would stop. I have flown in and out of DEN since the day it opened and have never been issued a clearance like this. I have always been instructed to hold short of ED when aircraft are landing Runway 17R. I believe this type of clearance should be avoided; specially in an area that is a hot spot. The aircraft behind us which received the same clearance had to confirm the clearance and the intent to hold short immediately after turning.
column123: We had a leg planned for DEN-ZZZ with Runways 17L; 17R; and 8 being used for departure.  Going westbound; I programmed and planned and briefed a 17R departure. The captain briefed a single-engine taxi.  After block out we were advised of a 17L departure.  For me that is several threats; a runway change requiring FMC programming; retrieving new performance; single-engine taxi ( increased workload; 2 engine-start flows etc); and unavoidable time when only the Captains eyes are outside the cockpit during FO single-engine duties during hi intensity ground operations @ DEN. On taxi out we were instructed to taxi to Runway 17L via Taxiway M and hold short of ED.  At some point northbound on Taxiway M with a hold short instruction in hand;  I initiated start of ENG 1.   During the engine start; the ground controller gave an additional instruction to turn right on to ED and hold short of the Runway 17R approach area.  Due to my required duties during an engine start which aren't easily paused/stopped; I could not write down the instruction; nor could I refer to my taxi diagram so that I could act as a backup-another set of eyes in the cockpit; I was only able to say-back the instruction and nothing else. The captain had a response to me that conveyed that he heard and understood the instruction.  I pressed on completing the engine-start.   I was heads down completing my duties during our expected right hand turn onto ED.  Shortly thereafter I heard the captain make a comment about an aircraft landing on Runway 17R and he brought the airplane to stop.  It was several moments before the ground controller came on the radio to say that we were to hold short of the approach area which was now behind us.  We were then instructed to continue taxi; switched over to tower; given a phone number and cleared for takeoff.

column121: While transitioning VNY Airport from the North I was told by ATC to remain on the east side of the 405 freeway while pattern traffic was restricted on the west side of the 405 (standard practice). The aircraft opposite direction never read back to remain on the west side of the 405 freeway and remained on the east side where I was causing a loss of separation. I took evasive action and immediately broke right around Aircraft Y which continued straight ahead. Had I not taken evasive action it would have been a midair collision. The pilot was a solo student pilot as well. I called the tower to ensure they spoke with the pilot and asked how to best mitigate this from happening again; we agreed the controller should have verified the read back and maybe in the future they will pay closer attention to closely separated planes.

column121: I was in the traffic pattern inbound for the second touch and go on Runway XX at ZZZ Airport. I was turning left base for Runway XX; before I noticed that an aircraft was inbound for left base for the opposite runway which is Runway XY. I tried to communicate with him that I was turning for the final of Runway XX but I did not hear any respond. Then I continued for the final of Runway XX. Then I called out for the short final of Runway YY then I corrected twice that I was on the short final of Runway XX. Upon the completion of the touch and go I called out that I was on the upwind of Runway XX and then the aircraft called out for left base of Runway XY. I pitched down to try to avoid the aircraft that was on the left base of Runway XY. I tried to see and avoid but I still did not have the aircraft in sight. I pitched up to climb and I saw the aircraft approximately 150 feet above me and I avoided the midair collision. column121: I was providing On the Job Instruction during the event. I had previously cleared Aircraft X to 17;000 feet prior to the trainee taking position. Another aircraft; Aircraft Y; was also receiving vectors for the approach and was given a descent to 14000 feet and a speed reduction to 210 knots. Aircraft X read back the instructions and then immediately Aircraft Y read back the instructions. On position; I did not hear the call sign of Aircraft X and thought that Aircraft Y read back the instruction a second time. It was clearly a different voice so I just thought that the pilots were confused as to who was talking and who was flying. A few moments later the Developmental recognized that Aircraft X was at 15;200 feet in a 15;500 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA) and issued a low altitude alert. The pilot responded that it was blocked and the trainee gave an immediate climb to 16;000 feet. I recognized the the trainee did not use the full proper phraseology by not saying the 'MVA in your area is XXX;' but decided at this point the solution had been acknowledge and it was not worth correcting the phraseology in a third transmission. The aircraft continued to descend to 14;400 feet until we observed a climb. During that time the aircraft crossed into a 14;900 foot MVA; but being in a climb I decided it was not necessary to issue another safety alert since we knew it was being corrected.There was a bit of frequency congestion due to confusion about the weather; and by the time I was ready to issue a brasher warning the aircraft was on a 10 mile final. I felt it was best to let the aircraft fly and have Local Control or Ground Control issue the brasher warning; so I coordinated that with Ground Control.

column121: I was on the ground at JNX with my student pilot getting ready for a soft field takeoff. The pattern was very congested with 5 aircraft in the pattern in various places. An aircraft had just landed; and there were a few other aircraft on downwind. A downwind aircraft stated that they were extending their downwind for departing traffic. I told my student that after the landing aircraft had exited the runway to be ready for immediate departure. The exiting aircraft called clear at this point and I told my student to make the radio call and take us out. As we were taxing out the aircraft that had said they were extending downwind was now on a 1.5 mile final. I told the student to accelerate and a radio call over the CTAF said go-around. I took the controls from my student to avoid a confliction with the aircraft going around. The aircraft going around side stepped to the right to avoid conflict. At no time was a G1000 traffic advisory issued; and no aircraft came close to contact. The overpopulated pattern with 4 aircraft doing stop and goes and my aircraft trying to find a spot to get out was a contributing factor to this issue. After the aircraft sidestepped; they immediately departed the pattern; and we continued our takeoff and entered left closed traffic to continue landing practice.
column123: Myself and a student who is currently working toward his CFI certificate went out for a training flight. After an hour of practicing maneuvers we approached JNX to practice takeoffs and landings. During our final approach into landing on Runway 21 at JNX my student made a call to let the other traffic in the pattern know of our position and our intentions; shortly after this call another plane pulled out on to the runway in frontof us. We made the decision to go-around and commenced a turn to the west so not to be on a collision course with the downwind traffic. We then proceeded to climb and leave the area.

column121: Holding short of Runway XX at ZZZ; our aircraft was given a departure clearance void time of 60 seconds from ZZZ Center. We initiated an immediate departure on Runway XX. After climbing via the obstacle departure procedure; we proceeded enroute and contacted ZZZ Center again. We then heard another aircraft call back to ZZZ Center; and stated they were forced to initiate a go-around as they were on final approach to Runway XX. It is my guess that our departure is what caused that aircraft to go-around. I however; do not recall seeing the aircraft on final approach; or hearing a radio call that the aircraft was on final approach. Conditions were VFR at the airport; but IFR traffic load was heavy. I do not recall ATC advising us of the aircraft on final approach. It again is responsibility of us as a crew to ensure the approach/runway was clear for takeoff. If this incident was caused by our departure; we should have more clearly made sure it was safe for takeoff; and will do so in the future.

column121: I released Aircraft X for departure from BFI in a south flow. Aircraft X reported climbing to 2;000 ft. on the NIRVANA1 SID. I radar identified Aircraft X and climbed them to 6;000 ft.. As Aircraft X was climbing through 1;800 ft. I noticed that Aircraft X appeared to be making a right turn towards the SEA VOR and not following the NIRVANA SID. I instructed Aircraft X to 'turn left immediately; left turn heading 130.' At this point; Aircraft X was starting to converge with Aircraft Y who was on final approach into SEA descending through 1;100 ft. on the ILS Approach to Runway 16R. Aircraft X was about 1.5 miles laterally separated and 700 ft. vertically separated from Aircraft Y and converging. I observed Aircraft X achieve vertical separation from Aircraft Y; then saw the turn to heading 130 start occurring. I gave Aircraft X the Brasher statement. The pilot of Aircraft X stated that it 'looks like the activation for the autopilot for the NIRVANA1 did not take place.'I honestly don't know how to fix this problem; but it is a recurring; systemic issue. This is not my first event with almost identical circumstances; and many of my co-workers have had very similar events. Attempts have already been made to alter the SID; or issue an initial heading for aircraft departing BFI; but these events still occur. The typical issue is that pilots are filing SEA.J5 and though BFI is issuing the NIRVANA1 SID and a Preferred Departure Route that would join J5 from TAOMA or SUMMA; pilot error including unchecked autopilot actions that result in a turn to SEA VOR will immediately cause convergence at the same altitude as aircraft inbound to SEA within the final approach fix; as well as any aircraft departing SEA during that time. There is very little and very subtle warning; and immediate action is necessary to recover separation and end the rapidly worsening danger of the situation. I believe in most cases it is a result of an individualized pilot deviation; and I don't know how to end those completely. The only other solution I can think of would be to completely remove/decommission the SEA VOR.

column121: I was cleared to transition underneath JFK's Class B Airspace; along the shoreline below 500 feet. while flying a westerly heading; I encountered wake turbulence from a plane landing on Runway 04R. Roughly 30 seconds before the encounter; I was cleared to climb; not leaving me adequate time to climb above the turbulence. The wake we experienced was stronger than expected. It shook our plane and dislodged the battery onboard my aircraft. I first noticed the acidic smell and shortly after observed smoke in the cockpit. I decided that I would need to land the plane immediately at JFK. After contacting Kennedy Tower; I got clearance to land on [Runway] 13R. Once acknowledging my clearance; I informed the Tower that I was shutting down my battery to prevent the fire from expanding and that we may lose further communication. We made a safe return to the airport and I stopped the plane on Taxiway P. I shut the motor down and deplaned my passengers. [Ground personnel] put out the fire and towed the plane to the FBO. Both myself and my passengers were evaluated by medical staff and were thankfully unscathed.

column121: I was conducting a discovery flight a requested flight following on the ground with a departure out to the southeast of the field near City X to fly over the house of the client. I was cleared to takeoff of Runway XX. On the climbout I was not sure if Tower wanted me to make a left or right turn towards the south east so I asked for clarification. He said 'you're going to the east right?' And I repeated stating we were going to the southeast. He replied back with instructions to make a right turn to head that way. Right as I turned onto the downwind; Tower switch me to Approach. I switched frequencies and began explaining something to the student as I started to cruise the plane out at 1;500 MSL. About 15 seconds after I switched frequencies to Approach; I gave them a call to check in telling them the altitude I was at and the direction I was headed; southeast. Immediately after I called in; I saw a King Air to my right that was supposedly on final for XX. They were on a track towards us but were in a climb. I saw they were climbing so I immediately started descending. After that is when Approach called in to tell me about the traffic saying that I was intercepting the final approach course for XX. When I looked at my ground track after the flight; I did drift slightly right when I was on the extended downwind; but I never intercepted the final approach course. I did not receive notice from the Tower about the King Air on final before being switched to Approach and it wasn't until after I checked in with Approach that they gave me the advisory. The King Air's TCAS system showed that we reached within 100 feet of each other but myself and the pilots in the King Air agreed that it was much more that 100 feet. I overall feel like there was a lack of communication from all parts that could have possibly prevented this occurrence. I personally could have been more specific with tower on where I was going. I could have been more watchful of my track to stay clear of final. The winds were from the northwest so that could have been a factor in why I was slightly drifting. That is why it is important to know where your winds are coming from to maintain a constant ground track along the ground and prevent drifting.

column121: During taxi to Runway 31L; ground control told us to hold short of the approach end of Runway 22 at Taxiway Z. As another aircraft was landing; ground told us to cross Runway 22 In front of the aircraft on landing roll and told that aircraft to give way to us. As we began to cross in front of the aircraft on landing roll; there was no indication that the aircraft was slowing down to give way to us. So I stopped our aircraft and the aircraft on landing roll passed in front of us. Ground said that we shouldn't have stopped.This event was caused by ground control setting up a disaster scenario by wanting us to cross in front of the aircraft on landing roll. If anything went wrong; like brake failure of the landing aircraft; then there would have been a potentially catastrophic aircraft accident.In the future; ground shouldn't cross aircraft in front of other aircraft on landing roll unless they are positive that the aircraft on landing roll has slowed down sufficiently to be safe.
column123: We were instructed to taxi from FBO to '[Runway] 31L via Zulu; hold short approach end [Runway] 22 at Zulu'. We exited the FBO ramp onto Zulu; turned left towards [Runway] 22 and noticed that [Taxiway] Zulu terminates before the approach end of [Runway] 22; and it becomes [Taxiway] Yankee. We discussed this on the flight deck that the taxi instructions should have been clearer because you can't really hold short of [Runway] 22 on Zulu; you would actually either hold short of [Runway] 22 approach on Yankee; or hold short of Yankee on Zulu. In any case; the Captain as PF (Pilot Flying) exercised some good judgement and decided to stop a bit back from Yankee; at least from the sign anyways. It's hard to figure where Zulu actually ended. That way we could respond to Ground if they modified their instructions to hold back further. Whatever is the case with Zulu or Yankee; we stopped and awaited further instructions. We noticed a bizjet (Aircraft Y) landing on [Runway] 4 so we suspected we were being held from crossing the approach end of [Runway] 22 (same as rollout end of [Runway] 4) due to the landing traffic. After a short wait; we heard ground instruct Aircraft Y which was now rolling out to 'give way to Aircraft X; he will be crossing at the end of the runway in front of you'. I cannot recall if the other aircraft acknowledged. The next thing we both heard was 'Aircraft X; cross Runway 22 and continue via Kilo'. I turned all the lights on and the Captain started to move forward. I looked right to clear and noticed the landing Aircraft Y was very close; it seemed to me he was past Bravo and Kilo 2 where I presumed he would have turned off; that his reversers were still deployed; and he was rolling very fast for so close to the end of the runway. I said 'STOP; we're not clear' or words to that effect and the Captain immediately stopped as he was now seeing Aircraft Y appear large in my windshield. It may have been hidden beside my windshield side pillar previously. We both agreed it would not have been pretty had we gone across the runway as it clearly appeared Aircraft Y was landing fast and rolling out long; and more importantly; evidently not giving way to us. After just a few seconds; Ground said 'Aircraft X; you're not moving' and we responded that it was not safe to do so. Ground replied; 'Aircraft Y is giving way to you' or words to that effect; and we replied that wasn't the case. After a few more seconds Aircraft Y exited onto Kilo in front of us; and Ground then instructed us to cross the runway and continue onto Kilo on our taxi to [Runway] 31L. Ground and Tower frequencies were combined per ATIS onto tower frequency 118.7; we were always using it; but when Aircraft Y cleared in front of us and we followed him until we turned left onto Charlie; we never heard him. Our subsequent taxi and departure was uneventful.The confusing or incorrect taxi instructions provided (i.e.; is it Zulu or Yankee to hold short) started us off with noticing ATC peculiarities as we spoke to Ground. The other aircraft not giving way to us; or appearing to do so; was the major cause of this averted conflict. Combining Tower and Ground on one frequency may also have caused some confusion; because the tower cab can hear everything but in the aircraft we can only actively hear the frequency we're currently using.First off; I think our alertness was the link in the chain that prevented an incident (or worse). I think that ATC made a procedural error in directing us to cross in front of the landing traffic without adequate time/distance to do so. I recommend [Company] request an official copy of the tape from the FAA if possible for review and development of lessons learned.

column121: Aircraft X arrived from the south east at 110 where ZZZ has a MVA (Minimum Vectoring Altitude) at the highest point of 115. I do not know if ZZZ1 has the same or lower MVA in that area. Per the LOA with ZZZ1 any arrivals in this area 130 or lower; must be on an airway and verbally coordinated. There was no coordination from ZZZ1.  OJTI was being conducted at the Approach position; I was the OS in the TRACON. Due to COVID social distancing requirements the OJTI was working from the 'B' scope; the trainee was working from the 'A' scope and I was at the OS desk. There is no radar scope or secondary monitor available at the OS desk to observe traffic; so I was unable to see the altitude of the aircraft. A regional jet that was about to be switched to the Tower told approach that an aircraft was trying to call approach and at that point I was able to observe Aircraft X at the altitude below the MVA. Aircraft X eventually was able to reach approach on radio and landed without incident.-Review ZZZ1-ZZZ LOA-Add a non-certified radar display at the ZZZ TRACON OS desk-Check the MVAs to the south east of ZZZ to see if there are any discrepancies between ZZZ1 and ZZZ MVAs.
column123: I am training the trainee on approach control. Three arrivals scheduled with close arrival times. Trainee accepted all three handoffs back to back well outside of our airspace boundary; further away than normal. He was not aware that Aircraft X was flying a wrong altitude for direction of flight as well as 500 feet below the MVA we have depicted on our maps until after accepting the handoff.  Neither of these two issues were coordinated between either facility. Aircraft landed without issue.We need to have matching MVA maps with ZZZ1. This could be a real issue in the future. That aircraft was 500 feet below our MVA and could not reach us on radio due to the terrain.
